title: Linked Lists
learningObjectives: Explain Linked Lists with interview-grade clarity
Implement Linked Lists solutions in clean Java
Choose the right pattern and justify complexity
Handle boundary cases without brittle logic
Use this topic to solve coding-round problems confidently

sections.title: Why Linked Lists matters
sections.content:
Linked-list questions test pointer reasoning more than syntax; one wrong pointer update can corrupt the entire structure.

This day is central to DSA interviews because it combines coding speed with reasoning quality. Interviewers usually evaluate three things:
1. Can you identify the right pattern quickly?
2. Can you explain why the approach is correct?
3. Can you reason about complexity and edge cases?

Pointer safety, traversal patterns, and in-place operations.

A high-quality answer should include:
- concise approach summary,
- small dry-run,
- complexity analysis,
- one alternative approach and trade-off.

By the end of Day 21, you should be able to solve representative problems and explain your decisions with confidence.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Linked Lists
sections.content:
### Why this topic matters now
Linked-list questions test pointer reasoning more than syntax; one wrong pointer update can corrupt the entire structure.

### Core interview mental model
For Linked Lists, first identify the problem family, then map it to a known pattern. Pattern-first thinking reduces random trial-and-error.

### Implementation guidance
1. Write the simplest correct version first.
2. Validate using one normal and one edge case.
3. Improve complexity only after correctness is locked.
4. Refactor to readable helper methods.

### Complexity and trade-off lens
Do not state complexity by memory. Derive it from operations performed. Mention time + space and where each cost comes from.

### Common failure mode
Most failures in Linked Lists are not syntax errors. They come from broken invariants, weak boundary checks, or unclear state tracking.

### Interview speaking template
- "I considered X because..."
- "The invariant is..."
- "Per iteration we do..., so complexity is..."
- "An alternative would be..., but I chose this due to..."

### Production relevance
Even outside interviews, these patterns improve throughput, latency, and correctness in backend data processing tasks.

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**Singly vs doubly linked:** deletion with only head pointer is O(n) singly; doubly with tail enables LRU designs.

**Dummy head** simplifies insertion at front — fewer null checks.

**Fast/slow pointers** detect cycles (Floyd) — know invariants: when they meet, resetting one to head finds cycle start.

**Memory:** each node is overhead vs array — cache locality worse; choose lists when frequent inserts in middle.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: Singly linked list: Node with next; traverse with a while loop.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Reverse linked list iteratively: three-pointer rewiring.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: Floyd cycle detection: slow/fast pointers to detect a loop.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Losing node references while reversing or deleting.
Forgetting null checks before accessing next pointers.
Mixing node identity and node value comparisons.
Incorrectly handling head/tail updates in edge cases.
Not drawing pointer state before coding complex operations.

sections.title: Exercise - Linked Lists Problem Drill
sections.problem:
Solve one medium-level Java problem for **Linked Lists**.

Requirements:
1. Provide brute-force and optimized approach.
2. Explain why optimized approach is better.
3. Include one edge-case test.
4. Print expected output for sample input.
5. State time/space complexity clearly.
hints: Dry-run on paper for one non-trivial case.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is a singly linked list node?
conceptual.answer: Object with value and reference to next—no random access by index.

When unsure, say what you would **measure** or **breakpoint-debug** before optimizing. Separate **compile-time** rules from **runtime** behavior in your explanation.

conceptual.question: How detect cycle?
conceptual.answer: Floyd’s tortoise/hare—fast moves 2 steps, slow 1; meeting implies cycle.

conceptual.answer: ArrayList cache-friendly; LinkedList node overhead—most apps prefer ArrayList unless proven need.

wrongAnswers: Linked list supports O(1) random access by index.
Floyd cycle detection always finds cycle length in first pass.
Java LinkedList is always faster than ArrayList for all workloads.
Reversing a list requires O(n) extra space always.
Merge sorted lists cannot be done recursively.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Day 21 Focus | Quick Reminder | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Linked Lists | Choose pattern before coding | Random brute-force wandering |
| Correctness | Invariant + edge checks | Dry-run once | Off-by-one / null-state miss |
| Complexity | Derive, do not guess | Mention time + space | Incomplete complexity answers |
| Communication | Explain decisions | Use trade-off statement | Generic textbook responses |
| Quality | Readable Java methods | Stable naming + small units | Clever but fragile logic |
